behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the real filesystem watcher + daemon wiring for recursive directory events. Mocked watcher callbacks can miss OS-specific recursive watch behavior and path normalization issues.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a temp input/output tree, create `input/sub/a.txt`, and assert transformed output appears. Use real disk events (no mocked watcher) and verify the pipeline runs once for the subdirectory file.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is primarily deterministic state/timing logic in the dedupe component. A focused unit test with a fake clock can expose real boundary-condition failures without OS or watcher infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedupe module directly: first request for `/x/a.txt` is accepted, second request within the configured window is rejected, and a request after the window is accepted. Drive time with a controllable clock.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The key risk is OS/watcher event burst behavior. Mocking event delivery can hide duplicate-event patterns that occur in real filesystems.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon on a real temp directory, perform a write pattern that commonly emits multiple events (create + append + close), and assert exactly one pipeline execution/output artifact for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real inode/path watcher behavior after directory deletion/recreation. Module-level mocks often miss re-subscription failures and stale handles.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real temp directory, delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering here depends on real file metadata and arrival concurrency, not just in-memory sorting. Mocked metadata can hide timestamp precision/resolution issues seen on real filesystems.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in the watched directory, record observed processing order, and assert it matches creation-time ordering from real file metadata (with deterministic tie-break rule if equal timestamps).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real write timing, event timing, and file readiness detection. Mocked I/O generally cannot reproduce partial-write races accurately.
behaviors.test_description: Open a file in the watched directory, write partial content, keep it open, and verify daemon does not process yet; then finish writing/close and assert processing occurs once with full final content.
